questions.stem: An alcoholic patient presents with palpitations, dizziness, and syncopal attacks. On examination, irregularly irregular pulse is seen. What will be seen on JVP?
explanation: The patient’s irregularly irregular pulse suggests atrial fibrillation (AF), which is common in chronic alcoholics (so-called holiday heart syndrome). In AF, the atria do not contract effectively → no coordinated atrial systole → no a wave is produced in the jugular venous pulse (JVP). Therefore, the finding will be absent a wave.
Jugular Venous Pulse (JVP) reflects right atrial pressure changes during the cardiac cycle.
The a wave corresponds to atrial contraction (just before S1).
In AF, there is complete loss of organized atrial contraction → a wave disappears.
Chronic alcoholics are prone to AF due to atrial dilation, electrolyte imbalance, and direct myocardial effects.
questions.subject: physiology

questions.stem: During the assessment of drinking water from a village tank, which of the following parameters is not within the acceptable limits for safe drinking water?
explanation: The correct answer is Cadmium - 0.03 mg/L because the BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) acceptable limit for cadmium in drinking water is 0.003 mg/L. A value of 0.03 mg/L is ten times higher and is therefore unsafe.
Parameter Acceptable Limit (mg/L) Cadmium 0.003 Fluoride 1.0 Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) 500 Nitrate 45
Cadmium – Toxic heavy metal, harmful to kidneys and bones.
Fluoride – 0.8 mg/L is safe and below the limit of 1.0 mg/L.
TDS – 300 mg/L is within safe range (limit 500 mg/L).
questions.subject: community_medicine

questions.stem: A 25-year-old female with a previous history of rheumatic fever. Examination shows a loud S1 and mid-diastolic murmur is having which of the following valvular heart disease?
explanation: The patient has a history of rheumatic fever and presents with a loud S1 and mid- diastolic murmur, which are classic findings of mitral stenosis. Rheumatic fever is the most common cause of MS worldwide.

Mitral stenosis results from thickening and fusion of the mitral valve leaflets, most often due to rheumatic heart disease. The loud S1 occurs because the stiff valve closes forcefully, and the mid-diastolic murmur is due to turbulent flow across the narrowed mitral orifice during diastole. Other features may include an opening snap and signs of pulmonary congestion.
Mitral stenosis:
Pathophysiology: Narrowed mitral orifice → ↑ left atrial pressure → pulmonary venous hypertension → right heart strain.
Clinical features: Dyspnea, orthopnea, hemoptysis, atrial fibrillation.

questions.stem: A patient presents to the ER with central chest pain. He has a past medical history of heart disease. ECG is shown below. What is the diagnosis? 8
explanation: ST-segment elevation:
Anterior leads: V2–V4
Lateral leads: I, aVL, V5, V6
Reciprocal ST-segment depression: leads III and aVF (inferior leads) Interpretation:
This combination indicates acute injury involving both the anterior wall (supplied by the LAD) and the lateral wall (supplied by the LCX or diagonal branches of LAD) — hence, anterolateral MI.

questions.stem: A study was conducted among nursing staff to analyze the time spent on different aspects of patient care, such as bed preparation, monitoring vital signs, attending the doctor’s rounds, blood sampling, and drug administration. Which of the following management techniques is most appropriate for this type of analysis?
explanation: The study in the question focuses on estimating how much time nursing staff spend on various activities across the day, but not by continuously timing each task from start to finish. Work Sampling is the appropriate method here because it involves taking observations at random intervals to determine the proportion of time spent on different activities.
Definition: A statistical technique for determining the proportion of time spent on various activities by making observations at random intervals.
Assess workload distribution.
Identify major time-consuming activities.
Provide data for staffing and scheduling decisions.
questions.subject: pharmacology

questions.stem: A patient after MI develops a broad QRS complex tachycardia. His BP is 120/85 mm Hg. ECG is given below. Best management?
explanation: Step 1: Recognising the rhythm
Broad QRS (>120 ms)
Regular tachycardia
Monomorphic appearance
Post-MI patient → high risk of ventricular tachycardia (VT)
questions.subject: physiology

questions.stem: A patient presents with weight gain, thinning of hair, and dry skin. Diagnosis is Hashimoto’s thyroiditis. Which antibody is seen in this case?
explanation: Hashimoto’s thyroiditis is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas, and it is strongly associated with anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies. These antibodies cause autoimmune destruction of the thyroid gland.

Anti-TPO antibodies target the enzyme thyroid peroxidase, which is essential for thyroid hormone synthesis (oxidation and iodination of iodine, coupling of iodotyrosines). Their presence confirms autoimmune thyroid disease, especially Hashimoto’s. Other antibodies, like anti- thyroglobulin, may also be present but anti-TPO has the highest diagnostic relevance.
Hashimoto’s thyroiditis:
AKA Chronic autoimmune lymphocytic thyroiditis.
Epidemiology: More common in women, especially middle-aged.
questions.subject: dermatology

questions.stem: An RTA patient has received 4 packed RBC within a few hours. Which of the following will be seen?
explanation: Stored packed RBCs contain citrate as an anticoagulant. Rapid transfusion leads to citrate binding ionized calcium in the recipient’s blood, causing acute hypocalcemia.

Citrate toxicity is more likely when large volumes of blood are transfused rapidly, as in massive transfusion protocols. The liver normally metabolizes citrate, but in shock or hypothermia this clearance is impaired.
Clinical signs: Perioral numbness, paresthesia, muscle cramps, tetany, hypotension, and prolonged QT interval on ECG.
Massive Blood Transfusion:
Definition: Replacement of blood volume exceeding patient’s total blood volume within 24 hrs (or >10 units of PRBC).

questions.stem: A young female presents with ptosis showing diurnal variation. CT chest shows an anterior mediastinal mass identified as thymus gland cancer. Which receptor is affected in this patient?
questions.option_c: Voltage-gated calcium channels at the NMJ
explanation: Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an autoimmune disorder in which antibodies target post-synaptic nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction, impairing neuromuscular transmission. Thymic abnormalities (hyperplasia or thymoma) are common in MG.

In MG, antibody-mediated blockade and destruction of post-synaptic ACh receptors reduce the number of available receptors, leading to muscle weakness. Weakness worsens with activity and improves with rest (diurnal variation). The association with thymoma is due to aberrant immune activation in the thymus.
Key features: Ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia, proximal muscle weakness, preserved reflexes, and response to acetylcholinesterase inhibitors.
Myasthenia Gravis:
Epidemiology: More common in young women and older men.
questions.subject: physiology

questions.stem: A 35-year-old female with progressive limb weakness for the past 1 year. On examination, muscle fasciculations and hypertonia are noted, and the sensory system is intact. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: ALS is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder involving both upper motor neurons (UMN) and lower motor neurons (LMN), leading to weakness, hypertonia, and fasciculations, while sparing the sensory system. The presence of UMN signs (hypertonia) and LMN signs (fasciculations) together is characteristic.
ALS affects motor neurons in the motor cortex, brainstem, and spinal cord.
UMN involvement → spasticity, hyperreflexia
LMN involvement → muscle atrophy, fasciculations, weakness.
Sensory pathways are usually preserved, helping differentiate from other neuropathies.
questions.subject: anatomy

questions.stem: A patient presents with symmetrical leg weakness and areflexia. He had developed diarrhoea 2 weeks ago. He was diagnosed as having GBS. What is the cause?
explanation: Guillain–Barré Syndrome (GBS) is an acute immune-mediated polyneuropathy, most often triggered by infections like Campylobacter jejuni. It primarily affects myelinated large- diameter motor and sensory fibers (A and B fibers), leading to weakness and areflexia.

A- and B-fibers are myelinated nerve fibers responsible for motor function and some sensory modalities. In GBS, autoantibodies and immune cells attack the myelin sheath, impairing conduction. This demyelination disrupts both motor signals (causing weakness) and reflex arcs (causing areflexia).
Guillain–Barré Syndrome:
Trigger: Often post-infectious (e.g., Campylobacter jejuni, CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma).
Pathophysiology: Autoimmune-mediated demyelination of peripheral nerves and nerve roots.
questions.subject: physiology

questions.stem: A patient has developed a cough and weight loss for the last 3 months. Biopsy of lung mass shows expression of chromogranin and synaptophysin. Which paraneoplastic syndrome will most likely occur in this patient?
explanation: Chromogranin and synaptophysin positivity suggests a neuroendocrine tumor, such as small cell carcinoma of the lung. These tumors often produce ectopic ADH, leading to syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH).

In SIADH, excessive ADH secretion causes water retention without corresponding sodium retention, resulting in dilutional hyponatremia and low plasma osmolality. Small cell lung carcinoma is notorious for secreting ectopic hormones, particularly ADH and ACTH. The presence of neuroendocrine markers confirms the tumor’s secretory nature.
Small Cell Carcinoma:
Origin: Neuroendocrine Kulchitsky cells in the lung.
Markers: Chromogranin, synaptophysin, neuron-specific enolase (NSE).
questions.subject: pathology

questions.stem: A patient presents with palpitations, headache, and sweating. His BP is 180/100 mmHg. 24-hour urinary metanephrine values are elevated. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Pheochromocytoma is a catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla. Classic symptoms are episodic headache, sweating, and palpitations with hypertension. Elevated 24-hour urinary metanephrines confirm catecholamine excess.
Pheochromocytomas arise from chromaffin cells derived from neural crest tissue.

Excess secretion of epinephrine and norepinephrine causes paroxysmal hypertension and sympathetic overactivity. Metanephrines are catecholamine metabolites, and their measurement in a 24-hour urine sample or plasma is the most sensitive test for diagnosis.
Pheochromocytoma:
Origin: Chromaffin cells (adrenal medulla).

questions.stem: A patient with osteoarthritis, for the last 3 months, has been taking ibuprofen and has developed occult GI bleeding. Which is correct about the cause?
questions.option_a: PGE-2 and prostacyclin production were reduced
questions.option_c: PGE-1 and prostacyclin production were reduced
questions.option_d: Mucosal injury due to inhibition of COX-2
explanation: NSAIDs like ibuprofen inhibit COX enzymes, reducing prostaglandin (PGE₂) and prostacyclin synthesis. These mediators normally maintain gastric mucosal protection by increasing mucus and bicarbonate secretion and promoting mucosal blood flow.

Loss of PGE₂ and prostacyclin leads to impaired gastric mucosal defense, making the mucosa susceptible to acid-induced injury and bleeding. This is the primary mechanism for NSAID-induced gastritis and peptic ulcers. COX-1 inhibition is particularly responsible for GI side effects, as it is involved in constitutive prostaglandin synthesis for mucosal protection.
NSAID-induced GI injury:
Mechanism: COX-1 inhibition → ↓ PGE₂, ↓ prostacyclin → ↓ mucus, ↓ bicarbonate, ↓ blood flow.
Risk factors: High-dose/long-term NSAID use, elderly, alcohol, corticosteroids, H.

questions.stem: What is the name of the technical platform developed under India’s National Tele Mental Health Programme (NTMHP) to deliver mental healthcare services through a multi-tiered system via telephone, digital platforms, and online portals?
explanation: The technical platform under India’s National Tele Mental Health Programme (NTMHP) designed specifically for delivering mental healthcare services through telephone, digital platforms, and online portals in a multi-tiered system is Tele-MANAS (Mental Health Assistance and Networking Across States).
Launched by: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India.
Provide accessible, affordable, and quality mental health services across India.
Reduce stigma and bridge the treatment gap for mental illnesses.
Tier 1: State Tele-MANAS cells with trained counsellors and mental health professionals answering calls via a toll-free helpline.

questions.stem: During a field visit to monitor training given to an ANM (Auxiliary Nurse Midwife) on safe immunization practices, you observe that she has used a syringe with a needle, a vaccine vial, and a cotton swab. After completing the immunization, which of the following statements about biomedical waste disposal is correct?
questions.option_a: The syringe with the needle should be disposed of in a black translucent container
questions.option_b: The used vaccine vial should be discarded in a blue bag
questions.option_c: A used cotton swab should be discarded in a red bag
questions.option_d: The needle should be bent and thrown in a red bag
explanation: Used or expired vaccine vials are considered glass waste and are disposed of in puncture- proof blue containers or blue bags for recycling or proper treatment.

Syringes with needles go into white translucent puncture-proof containers without removing the needle, cotton swabs with blood/body fluids go in yellow bags, and needles should never be bent or broken before disposal, as it increases the risk of needle-stick injury.

Item Waste Category Disposal Container/Bag Syringe with needle Sharps White translucent container (puncture- proof) Needle not removed, container disinfected before disposal Used vaccine vial (glass) Glass waste Blue puncture-proof box/bag After ensuring vial is empty/expired Cotton swab (contaminated) Infectious Yellow bag If with blood/body fluids Plastic syringe without needle Contaminated recyclable Red bag For autoclaving/microwaving before recycling Biomedical Waste Management Summary

Fetus, organ, tissue, soiled cotton, gauze, dressings, bandages, casts, plasters, IUD, expired/discarded medicines, cytotoxic waste, body secretions e.g. sputum, pus, feces, X- ray developer fluids, disinfectants, linens, beddings, mattress, gowns, culture plates, blood bags, blood transfusion sets, live vaccine vial, blood products, masks, shoe cover, head cover: Yellow bag for incineration, plasma pyrolysis, and deep burial.
Recyclable plastics, tubes, vacutainers without blood, syringes without needles, urobags, catheters, Ryle's tubes, IV drip sets, face shields, goggles: Red bag.
questions.subject: community_medicine

questions.stem: Who is primarily responsible for registering eligible couples for family planning and maintaining their records under the national health system?
explanation: Under India’s national health system, the Multipurpose Worker (Female), often synonymous with the Auxiliary Nurse Midwife (ANM), is responsible for registering eligible couples for family planning and maintaining their records.

This includes updating the Eligible Couple Register, tracking contraceptive use, and ensuring follow-up services. ASHAs and Anganwadi Workers assist in community mobilization and education but do not maintain the official eligible couple records.
Multipurpose Worker (Female) / ANM in Family Planning:
Role in Family Planning:
Maintain Eligible Couple Register (ECR) at the sub-centre level.

questions.stem: A HIV-positive patient presents with chronic diarrhoea for 4 weeks. Stool microscopy shows oval/ elliptical cysts measuring 16 X 20 μm. Diagnosis is?
explanation: Cystoisospora (formerly Isospora belli) causes chronic diarrhea in immunocompromised patients like those with HIV. Its oocysts are oval/elliptical, acid- fast, and measure around 20–30 μm (commonly ~16 × 20 μm).

Cystoisospora belli infection leads to malabsorptive diarrhea, often chronic in HIV patients. Diagnosis is made via stool microscopy or modified acid-fast staining, showing large oval oocysts. The organism is transmitted via ingestion of sporulated oocysts in contaminated food or water. Treatment typically involves trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX).
Opportunistic Protozoal GI infections in HIV:
+ + Treatment Nitazoxanide Cotrimoxazole Cotrimoxazole Incorrect Options:
Cryptosporidium (Option A): Much smaller oocysts (4–6 μm); typically causes acute watery diarrhea.

questions.stem: A known case of hyperthyroidism, who is not on medication, is scheduled for total thyroidectomy. The PAC check was not done properly. What is the leading complication that can occur during surgery?
explanation: In uncontrolled hyperthyroidism, catecholamine sensitivity is increased, predisposing the patient to severe intraoperative arrhythmias, especially during surgical manipulation of the thyroid gland.

A patient with untreated hyperthyroidism undergoing thyroidectomy is at high risk of thyroid storm, precipitated by surgical stress. This can manifest with tachyarrhythmias, hyperthermia, and hypertension. Arrhythmia (especially atrial fibrillation or supraventricular tachycardia) is the most immediate and dangerous intraoperative complication.
Proper preoperative control with antithyroid drugs, β-blockers, and adequate preparation is essential.
Hyperthermia (Option A): Can occur in thyroid storm but is not the leading intraoperative danger; arrhythmia is more acutely life-threatening.
Hypothermia (Option B): Not typically associated with hyperthyroidism; more a concern in prolonged surgeries in cold OR environments.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old male presents with deep-seated retro-orbital pain of cluster headache. What is the best management?
explanation: High-flow 100% oxygen is the most effective acute abortive therapy for cluster headaches, rapidly relieving pain within 15–20 minutes in most cases. It is safe, well- tolerated, and works faster than most medications.

Cluster headaches are severe unilateral headaches with autonomic features. Acute attacks require rapid-acting treatment — high-flow oxygen via a non-rebreather mask or subcutaneous sumatriptan are first-line.

Oxygen relieves pain by causing vasoconstriction and possibly modulating trigeminal- autonomic reflexes. Prophylaxis (e.g., verapamil, lithium) is started later to prevent recurrences, but during an acute attack, oxygen is the gold standard.
Cluster Headache:
Verapamil (Option A): Used for prophylaxis, not acute attack.
questions.subject: ent

questions.stem: Patient presents with hypertension and hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. CT scan shows a mass over the kidney with elevated ARR. What is the next best step for the management of this patient?
explanation: This patient likely has primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn’s syndrome) due to an adrenal adenoma. In unilateral disease confirmed by imaging and biochemistry (elevated Aldosterone–Renin Ratio), surgical adrenalectomy is the treatment of choice.

Hypertension + hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis + high ARR + adrenal mass suggests unilateral aldosterone-producing adenoma. After confirming lateralization (often with adrenal venous sampling in some protocols), the definitive treatment is laparoscopic adrenalectomy. Medical management with mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone/eplerenone) is reserved for bilateral adrenal hyperplasia or non-surgical candidates.
Primary Hyperaldosteronism:
Spironolactone (Option A): First-line for bilateral hyperplasia or non-surgical patients, not preferred for unilateral adenoma in a surgical candidate.
ACTH stimulation test (Option C): Used for adrenal insufficiency diagnosis, not hyperaldosteronism.
questions.subject: medicine

questions.stem: A 35-year-old male has had chronic hypercalcemia for the last 1.5 years, but PTH levels are normal. Which of the following is most likely the cause?
explanation: From the given history, the patient has chronic hypercalcemia but PTH is normal. This means the hypercalcemia is PTH-independent. Sarcoidosis, due to extra-renal production of calcitriol by activated macrophages, presents with hypercalcemia.

Multisystem granulomatous disease of unknown etiology, characterized by non-caseating granulomas. In sarcoidosis, activated macrophages inside the granulomas express 1-α- hydroxylase, converting 25-hydroxyvitamin D into 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol). This leads to increased intestinal calcium absorption and persistent hypercalcemia.
Because the calcium level is high, the parathyroid gland decreases PTH secretion, explaining the normal or low PTH levels in such cases.

Malignancy (Option A) – Can cause hypercalcemia via PTHrP or bone metastasis, but presentation is usually acute and associated with systemic symptoms like weight loss; chronic stable picture is less likely.
Parathyroid hyperplasia (Option C) – Would cause elevated PTH, not normal PTH.
questions.subject: ent

questions.stem: On examination, a person has distended neck veins, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, and a shift of the trachea. What is the management? 43
questions.option_a: Bedside CXR in casualty followed by chest tube insertion
questions.option_b: Wide bore needle in 2 nd ICS
explanation: This is tension pneumothorax – caused by air trapping in the pleural space under pressure.
Respiratory distress
Hyperresonance (air in pleural space)
Absent breath sounds (no lung expansion)
Distended neck veins (↑ intrathoracic pressure → ↓ venous return)
questions.subject: anatomy

questions.stem: A female presents with amenorrhea and galactorrhea. Serum prolactin is elevated. MRI shows pituitary adenoma. Best management for this patient?
explanation: The patient has hyperprolactinemia due to a pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma).
Hyperprolactinemia → suppresses GnRH → ↓ LH/FSH → menstrual irregularities and infertility.

First-line treatment for prolactinomas is dopamine agonists (Bromocriptine or Cabergoline) as they inhibit prolactin secretion and can shrink the tumor. Bromocriptine is safe in women desiring pregnancy.
Surgery (trans-sphenoidal resection) is reserved for drug-resistant or compressive symptoms (e.g., visual loss).
Pituitary adenoma (most common)
questions.subject: obgy

questions.stem: A child with progressive pallor and bone has an elevated HbS based on the HPLC report. Which is the best treatment to manage hemolysis in this patient?
explanation: HPLC (High-Performance Liquid Chromatography) showing elevated HbS indicates Sickle Cell Disease (SCD). SCD is due to a mutation in β-globin gene → HbS polymerizes under low oxygen → sickling → chronic hemolysis + vaso-occlusive crises.

Voxelotor is an HbS polymerization inhibitor that increases hemoglobin’s affinity for oxygen, stabilizing the oxygenated form and reducing RBC sickling. This directly reduces hemolysis and improves anemia, making it the best treatment here for hemolysis control.
Sickle Cell Disease (SCD):
Pathophysiology: Point mutation (Glu → Val) in β-chain of Hb → polymerization in deoxygenated state → distorted RBCs → hemolysis + microvascular occlusion.
Clinical features:
questions.subject: anatomy

questions.stem: What is the probable diagnosis for a patient who exhibits miosis, anhidrosis, mild ptosis, and a persistent small pupil even in low light conditions?
explanation: The clinical presentation of miosis, anhidrosis, mild ptosis, and a persistent small pupil even in low light is suggestive of Horner syndrome. Ptosis is a result of the weakness of Müller muscle and miosis due to the unopposed action of the sphincter pupillae with resultant anisocoria.

Pancoast tumor can compress the sympathetic nerves, leading to the classic triad of symptoms seen in Horner syndrome: ptosis, miosis (constricted pupil), and ipsilateral facial anhidrosis (lack of sweating).
Acquired Neurogenic Ptosis: Acquired ptosis with drooping of the upper eyelid caused by a disruption in the nerve supply to the muscles responsible for elevating the eyelid.

Third Cranial (Oculomotor) Nerve Palsy: The third nerve can be damaged by trauma, ischemia, infection, tumor, or compression, causing ptosis and diplopia.Oculomotor nerve palsy causes ptosis as it supplies the levator palpebrae superioris muscle.
The ptotic eyelid can be elevated with a frontalis sling or levator aponeurosis advancement.
questions.subject: ophthalmology

questions.stem: A 60-year-old female presents with weakness, back pain and repeated infections. Work up shows M spike on serum electrophoresis. Prognosis is determined by which of the following factors?
explanation: The International Staging System (ISS) for multiple myeloma — a widely accepted prognostic model — uses two lab values: serum β₂-microglobulin and serum albumin.
β₂-Microglobulin is a marker of tumor burden and renal function; elevated levels indicate more advanced disease and poorer prognosis.
Albumin reflects the patient’s general health and disease activity; lower levels are associated with more aggressive disease and worse outcomes.

ISS Staging Stage Criteria Prognosis I β₂-microglobulin < 3.5 mg/L albumin ≥ 3.5 g/dL Best prognosis II Intermediate values (not fitting I or III) Intermediate prognosis III Intermediate values (not fitting I or III) Poor prognosis Incorrect Options:
IgG and albumin (Option A): While IgG is commonly the paraprotein in multiple myeloma, it is not part of the ISS prognostic criteria.
questions.subject: ent

questions.stem: A young male patient has the following serological status: HbsAg positive, IgM anti Hbc negative, IgG Anti-Hbc positive and HBeAg positive. Select the best treatment for this patient?
explanation: The serology indicates chronic active hepatitis B infection:
HBsAg positive → Ongoing HBV infection.
IgM anti-HBc negative, IgG anti-HBc positive → Chronic (not acute) phase.
HBeAg positive → High viral replication and infectivity.

Patients with chronic active HBV and high viral load (HBeAg positive) require antiviral therapy to suppress viral replication and reduce liver inflammation, fibrosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma risk.

questions.stem: A patient with RTA is passing red color urine due to myoglobinuria. Which of the following will most likely be seen in this patient?
explanation: Myoglobinuria typically occurs after rhabdomyolysis, where muscle breakdown releases myoglobin and intracellular potassium into the bloodstream.
Myoglobin is nephrotoxic, causing acute tubular necrosis (ATN).
Tubular injury in ATN often impairs potassium excretion, resulting in Type 4 RTA physiology, which is characterized by hyperkalemia and mild metabolic acidosis.
The potassium rise is further exacerbated by direct release from damaged muscle cells.
Hypernatremia (Option A): Less common in ATN; if anything, sodium tends to be low due to renal losses and fluid shifts.

questions.stem: A patient after RTA had a renal shut down. Work-up shows: FeNa: < 1%, Urine osmolality: 300 mOsm/kg, Urinary sodium: 40 mEq/L. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This presentation points to intrinsic renal injury, specifically ATN, where damaged renal tubules lose the ability to reabsorb sodium and concentrate urine.
Key features in this case:
Urinary sodium > 40 mEq/L → tubular sodium wasting
Urine osmolality ~300 mOsm/kg → isosthenuria (urine similar to plasma)
FeNa < 1% is more typical of pre-renal AKI, but in early ATN or mixed states it can also be low.
questions.subject: physiology

questions.stem: A patient is in ICU and there is a failed attempt of subclavian catheterization. Shortly after, BP drops and the pulse rises. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Subclavian vein catheterization carries a significant risk of pleural injury, which can cause pneumothorax.
Air entry into the pleural space leads to reduced venous return, causing hypotension and reflex tachycardia.
In the ICU setting, this can rapidly progress to tension pneumothorax, which is life- threatening and requires urgent decompression.
Typical signs: sudden drop in BP, tachycardia, respiratory distress, unilateral decreased breath sounds, hyperresonance on percussion.
Displacement of ET (Option B): Would cause hypoxia and respiratory distress, but is not directly related to subclavian line attempt.

questions.stem: A patient had a road traffic accident (RTA). NCCT head was normal on admission. The patient later died after several days in the ICU due to coma. Brain biopsy revealed multiple punctate hemorrhages. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Diffuse axonal injury is a primary brain injury caused by shearing forces during high- velocity trauma (e.g., RTA).
These forces disrupt axons in the white matter, corpus callosum, and brainstem.
On initial imaging, NCCT may be normal or show only tiny petechial hemorrhages; the condition is often diagnosed clinically or via MRI.
Histopathology (or biopsy) reveals multiple punctate hemorrhages in deep white matter tracts.
DAI often leads to immediate or prolonged coma without a lucid interval and carries a high mortality rate.
questions.subject: pathology

questions.stem: A patient presents with fever and altered sensorium. Peripheral smear shows multiple ring stages. Best treatment is?
explanation: Fever with altered sensorium in malaria indicates cerebral malaria — a severe form most often due to Plasmodium falciparum.
Multiple ring forms on smear strongly suggest falciparum malaria.
Artesunate is preferred over quinine for severe malaria as per WHO guidelines, given its better safety profile, faster parasite clearance, and lower mortality.
It is administered intravenously in the acute setting.
Primaquine (Option A): Used for radical cure of P. vivax and P. ovale to target hypnozoites in the liver, not for acute severe falciparum malaria.
questions.subject: ent

questions.stem: Which is the best confirmatory method to ensure the central line is in the jugular vein?
explanation: Importance of Chest X-ray in Central Line Placement
Initial Assessment:
A chest X-ray should be performed immediately after internal jugular and subclavian line placement to confirm correct catheter placement and rule out iatrogenic pneumothorax.
Post-Procedure Verification:
It is crucial to verify catheter placement and ensure no complications, like pneumothorax, following the procedure.
questions.subject: anatomy

questions.stem: What defect is seen in Bernard Soulier syndrome?
explanation: Bernard–Soulier syndrome is a rare autosomal recessive platelet disorder characterized by a defect in platelet adhesion due to deficiency or dysfunction of the platelet membrane glycoprotein Ib (GpIb) complex, which is the receptor for von Willebrand factor (vWF).
This defect prevents platelets from binding to exposed subendothelial vWF during vascular injury, leading to defective primary hemostasis.
Clinically, it presents with mucocutaneous bleeding, prolonged bleeding time, and thrombocytopenia with giant platelets on peripheral smear.
Deficiency of GpIIb/IIIa receptor (Option A): Seen in Glanzmann thrombasthenia — causes defective platelet aggregation, not adhesion.
Deficiency of von Willebrand factor (Option C): Seen in von Willebrand disease — results in both defective platelet adhesion and secondary coagulation defect due to reduced factor VIII stability.
questions.subject: biochemistry

questions.stem: A patient presents with SOB and fatigue. CXR was done. What is the diagnosis? 62
explanation: Asbestosis is an interstitial lung disease caused by inhalation of asbestos fibers, often in occupations such as shipbuilding, construction, or insulation work.
The hallmark radiologic finding is calcified pleural plaques, especially along the parietal pleura and diaphragm (“peripheral pleural calcification”), along with lower lobe interstitial fibrosis.
Patients present with progressive dyspnea and restrictive lung disease on PFTs.
TB (Option A): More commonly involves apical regions, with cavitation and infiltrates; pleural calcifications are uncommon.
Silicosis (Option C): Typically shows upper lobe nodules and “eggshell” hilar node calcification.

questions.stem: A patient presents with progressive shortness of breath for the last 6 months, accompanied by dry cough. Auscultation reveals bilateral basal end-inspiratory crepitations. There is no history of fever, joint pain, or occupational exposure. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF) is a chronic, progressive fibrosing interstitial lung disease of unknown cause, typically seen in older adults.
The classic presentation includes gradually worsening exertional dyspnoea and nonproductive cough over months.
On auscultation, bilateral basal end-inspiratory “Velcro” crackles are characteristic.
Absence of fever, joint symptoms, or occupational dust/fume exposure supports an idiopathic diagnosis rather than secondary interstitial lung disease.

Cor-pulmonale (Option A): Refers to right ventricular hypertrophy and failure secondary to chronic lung disease or pulmonary hypertension; it is a consequence, not the primary cause, of the lung findings here.

questions.stem: A patient has a long arm span, hypermobile joints and hypermobile lens. What is the defective protein?
explanation: The presentation describes Marfan syndrome, a connective tissue disorder caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene, leading to defective fibrillin-1, an essential component of microfibrils that provide structural integrity to connective tissue and regulate TGF-β signaling.

Clinical features include tall stature, disproportionately long limbs (increased arm span- to-height ratio), joint hypermobility, lens subluxation (typically upward), and cardiovascular involvement (aortic root dilatation, aneurysm).

Collagen (Option B): Defects cause disorders such as Ehlers–Danlos syndrome and osteogenesis imperfecta, which may have joint hypermobility but not the full Marfan phenotype with lens dislocation and long arm span.
Elastin (Option C): Deficiency is associated with conditions like supravalvular aortic stenosis and Williams syndrome, but not the Marfan skeletal and ocular features.
Keratin (Option D): Structural protein in epithelial cells; mutations lead to skin and hair disorders, not connective tissue or cardiovascular manifestations.
questions.subject: ophthalmology

questions.stem: A child presents with kawasaki disease with multiple small coronary artery aneurysms. Treatment is?
explanation: In Kawasaki disease patients with small coronary artery aneurysms, low-dose aspirin is continued for about 6 to 8 weeks to prevent thrombosis as the aneurysms may regress over time.
The 6-week duration covers the period of highest thrombotic risk during vascular remodeling.
Early IVIG administration is also critical during the acute phase to reduce aneurysm Incorrect Options:
Aspirin lifelong (Option A): Reserved for large or giant aneurysms with persistent risk of thrombosis.
Aspirin for 4 weeks (Option B): Typically used when no coronary involvement is present.
questions.subject: anatomy

questions.stem: The infant is being evaluated for recurrent episodes of seizures. ECG shows Hypsarrhythmia. Which drug is preferred for management?
explanation: Hypsarrhythmia on EEG is characteristic of infantile spasms (West syndrome), a severe epileptic encephalopathy in infants.
The first-line treatment is adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), which helps reduce spasms and improve developmental outcomes.
Vigabatrin (Option B): Also effective, especially in infantile spasms associated with tuberous sclerosis, but ACTH remains the preferred initial therapy in most cases.
Valproate (Option C): Used for other seizure types, but less effective for infantile spasms.
Carbamazepine (Option D): Not effective and may worsen infantile spasms.
questions.subject: physiology

questions.stem: A patient presents with contralateral face, arm, and leg weakness. NCCT shows intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH). What is the most likely location of the CNS bleed?
explanation: Intracerebral hemorrhages causing contralateral hemiparesis (weakness of face, arm, and leg) most commonly occur in the basal ganglia, especially the putamen.
The basal ganglia hemorrhage compresses the internal capsule where corticospinal fibers run, causing dense contralateral motor deficits.
Midbrain (Option B): Lesions here often cause oculomotor nerve palsy and contralateral weakness but are less common sites for primary ICH.
Pons (Option C): Hemorrhages can cause "locked-in" syndrome or quadriplegia, often accompanied by cranial nerve signs, but less isolated hemiparesis.
Medulla (Option D): Hemorrhages here are rare; symptoms include contralateral limb weakness with ipsilateral cranial nerve deficits (Wallenberg syndrome) but not classic hemiparesis alone.

questions.stem: What is the management of undulant fever?
explanation: Undulant fever is a classic presentation of brucellosis, a zoonotic infection.
The standard treatment involves combination therapy with doxycycline (100 mg twice daily for 6 weeks) plus rifampicin (600–900 mg daily for 6 weeks) to prevent relapse and ensure eradication.
Monotherapy with doxycycline alone is insufficient due to high relapse rates.
Doxycycline alone (Option A): Higher risk of relapse if used without a companion drug.
Doxycycline with azithromycin (Option C): Not a standard regimen for brucellosis.

questions.stem: A patient has cheilosis with koilonychia and anemia. Diagnosis is?
explanation: Plummer-Vinson syndrome is characterized by the triad of iron deficiency anemia, esophageal webs, and glossitis/cheilosis.
Koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails) and cheilosis (cracking at the corners of the mouth) are classic signs of iron deficiency.
This syndrome increases the risk of developing esophageal squamous cell carcinoma if untreated.
Esophageal cancer (Option B): May develop as a complication of Plummer-Vinson syndrome but is not a presenting diagnosis with these features.
Achalasia cardia (Option C): Presents with dysphagia and esophageal motility disorder, but without anemia or cheilosis.

questions.stem: Which classification is used for Barrett esophagus?
explanation: The Prague C & M classification is used for Barrett esophagus, where C denotes the circumferential extent of the Barrett mucosa and M denotes the maximal extent from the gastroesophageal junction, as measured endoscopically.
It helps in standardizing reporting and guiding surveillance.
Prague Classification
Two key measurements:
C (Circumferential extent)

questions.stem: A female patient complains of chest pain while waking up in the morning. Pain is more in winter months. Which of the following best describes this patient?
questions.option_c: Subendocardial ischemia due to increased demand of myocardium
questions.option_d: Increased sympathomimetic drive in morning hours
explanation: This presentation is classic for Prinzmetal’s (variant) angina, which is due to transient vasospasm of coronary arteries often occurring at rest, particularly in the early morning hours and more frequently in cold weather.
Acetylcholine and other vasoconstrictors can trigger the spasm.
Fixed plaque obstruction (Option A): Seen in stable angina due to atherosclerosis; pain is usually exertional, not at rest.
Subendocardial ischemia due to increased demand of myocardium (Option C): Describes the mechanism in stable angina during exercise or stress, not rest-related vasospasm.
Increased sympathomimetic drive in morning hours (Option D): Plays a role in acute coronary syndromes but doesn’t fully explain the cold-weather, early-morning, rest-related pain due to vasospasm.

questions.stem: CSF shows abundant PMNs with low sugar and elevated proteins. Diagnosis is?
explanation: Acute bacterial meningitis (ABM) is characterized by neutrophilic predominance (PMNs) in CSF, low glucose (due to bacterial consumption and impaired transport), and elevated proteins (due to increased permeability of the blood–brain barrier).
CSF Findings in various types of meningitis:

Openin Pressur Predominant Cell Protein Glucose Appearanc Bacterial Meningitis Elevated Polymorphonuclea r Neutrophils (PMNs) Elevate Decrease Turbid Fungal/Tubercula r Meningitis Elevated Lymphocytes Elevate Decrease Straw- colored (TB meningitis) Viral Meningitis Normal Slightly Elevated Lymphocytes Normal Slightly Elevate Normal Normal or Turbid Incorrect Options:

Tubercular Meningitis (Option B): Usually shows lymphocytic predominance, very low glucose, and high protein; PMN predominance may occur early but is not typical for established Viral encephalitis (Option C): Typically has lymphocytic predominance, normal or mildly reduced sugar, and mild–moderate protein elevation.
Viral meningitis (Option D): Similar to viral encephalitis in CSF findings — lymphocytic predominance and normal glucose.

questions.stem: A 5 year old boy presented with hematemesis and was found to have splenomegaly, on examination. There was a past history of exchange transfusion in this child for neonatal jaundice. What is the probable diagnosis?
explanation: The presentation of hematemesis and splenomegaly, along with the significant history of exchange transfusion in the neonatal period, strongly suggests portal vein thrombosis.
This condition is a common cause of portal hypertension in children and can result from umbilical vein catheterization during exchange transfusion.

Splenic vein thrombosis (Option A)  is also a possibility in a case of splenomegaly, but the history of exchange transfusion suggests a more generalized thrombotic event involving the portal Budd-Chiari syndrome (Option C) is a condition characterized by the obstruction of hepatic veins, leading to liver congestion. It is less likely in this case, as the presentation of hematemesis and splenomegaly is more suggestive of portal vein involvement.
Liver cirrhosis(Option D): Although it can cause portal hypertension, cirrhosis is uncommon in a 5-year-old without a history of chronic liver disease.
questions.subject: paediatrics

questions.stem: A skin graft was performed on an immunocompromised patient using tissue from another immunocompromised individual. Weeks later, the patient developed contractures and systemic symptoms. What is the most likely cause?
questions.option_c: Rejection due to recipient T8 cells
explanation: Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) occurs when immunocompetent donor T lymphocytes recognize recipient tissues as foreign and mount an immune response.
This typically happens in immunocompromised recipients who are unable to reject the donor T cells.
In the context of a skin graft (especially from another immunocompromised individual where donor lymphocytes may survive),
GVHD can cause systemic symptoms (fever, rash, liver dysfunction, diarrhea) along with local changes like skin contractures.
Graft failure (Option B): Results from the inability of the graft to establish blood supply, leading to necrosis — it occurs early, not weeks later, and does not cause systemic immune symptoms.
questions.subject: dermatology

questions.stem: The finding seen in the image is? 81
explanation: Herbert’s pits are small, shallow depressions seen at the limbal conjunctiva due to healed limbal follicles in trachoma.
They are considered pathognomonic of previous trachoma infection.
Pathophysiology: Chronic Chlamydia trachomatis infection causes limbal follicles → ulceration → healing with depression.
WHO grading: Presence of Herbert’s pits indicates prior active trachoma and is seen in the scarring stage (TS).
Horner-Trantas spots (Option A): White, chalky dots at limbus composed of eosinophils in vernal keratoconjunctivitis, not trachoma.

questions.stem: A patient with pituitary adenoma compressing the optic chiasma now presents with loss of visual field. What visual field defect will be seen in the patient?
explanation: The optic chiasm contains crossing fibers from the nasal retina of both eyes.
Pituitary adenoma commonly compresses the central chiasm from below → affects crossing nasal fibers → loss of temporal visual fields in both eyes → Bitemporal hemianopia
Clinical scenario: Gradual, progressive peripheral vision loss, more noticeable while driving or walking.
Mechanism: Nasal retinal fibers detect temporal fields → damage leads to bitemporal hemianopia.
Visual Pathway Lesions and Defects Site of Lesion Fibers Affected Field Defect Optic Nerve Ipsilateral Temporal + Nasal Fibers Optic Chiasma Bilateral Nasal Fibers
questions.subject: ophthalmology

questions.stem: A patient presents with complaints of eye strain. The given image shows the focus of light rays in different meridians in the eye. Which refractive error is present in this patient? 87
explanation: The image of Sturm’s conoid demonstrates that parallel light rays do not converge to a single focal point, but rather form two focal lines at different distances due to unequal refractive power in different meridians of the cornea or lens.

This optical phenomenon is characteristic of astigmatism, where light rays along one meridian focus in front of the retina while rays along the perpendicular meridian focus either behind or at a different point.
Astigmatism causes blurred or distorted vision and eye strain (asthenopia), especially with prolonged near tasks.
Sturm’s conoid in Astigmatism:
1. Light rays from an object pass through two principal meridians with different curvatures.
questions.subject: ophthalmology

questions.stem: A myopic patient presents with complaints of flashes and floaters. On examination, a deep anterior chamber is seen. What is the likely diagnosis?
explanation: A myopic patient with flashes (photopsia), floaters, and a deep anterior chamber suggests posterior vitreous detachment with retinal tear. This allows liquefied vitreous to pass into the subretinal space, leading to rhegmatogenous retinal detachment.

Key mechanism: Myopia → long axial length → retinal thinning + lattice degeneration → predisposes to retinal breaks. These breaks allow fluid entry under retina → separation from retinal pigment epithelium.
Deep anterior chamber in this context is due to elongation of the globe and posterior shift of the lens–iris diaphragm in high myopia.
Type of Retinal Detachment Clinical Features Rhegmatogenous Retinal Detachment 1. Floaters (dark spots) due to rapid vitreous degeneration.
2. Photopsia (flashes of light) caused by retinal irritation from vitreous movements.

questions.stem: An elderly female presents with sudden onset of pain, redness, and decreased vision. On examination, hazy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, and shallow anterior chamber are noted. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: The presence of ocular pain, redness, decreased vision, and corneal haze can be seen in both acute uveitis and acute angle-closure glaucoma, but here the combination of inflammation signs (ciliary congestion, corneal edema) and pupil changes points toward acute uveitis as the answer given.
In uveitis, cells and flare in anterior chamber cause corneal haze, and pupil is irregular or sluggishly reactive due to posterior synechiae; early on it may appear mid- dilated.
The shallow anterior chamber here may be due to ciliary body swelling pushing the iris forward in acute inflammation.
Key features of Acute Uveitis:
1. Pain, photophobia, redness (ciliary flush).
questions.subject: ophthalmology

questions.stem: A patient presents with a painless ulcer in the eye. On examination, a long, branching ulcer with desquamated epithelium is seen on the cornea. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: A painless corneal ulcer indicates reduced or absent corneal sensation.
The finding of a long, branching ulcer with desquamated epithelium but without the typical raised edges and terminal bulbs of herpes simplex keratitis suggests a neurotrophic ulcer.
Neurotrophic keratopathy results from damage to trigeminal nerve sensory supply, leading to impaired healing and epithelial breakdown, producing branching epithelial defects.
Neurotrophic Ulcer:
1. Painless despite corneal epithelial defect.
questions.subject: ophthalmology

questions.stem: A 45-year-old patient with eye examination findings of a deep anterior chamber and jet-black pupil is prescribed +12D glasses. Likely diagnosis?
explanation: Clinical reasoning:
Deep anterior chamber → suggests absence of crystalline lens.
Jet-black pupil → no lens reflections seen.
+12D prescription → large convex lens to replace lost refractive power of natural lens.
Aphakia = complete absence of the natural lens (usually post-cataract surgery without IOL implantation).
questions.subject: ophthalmology

questions.stem: A man presents to the hospital with a bizarre behavior. He is unable to recall his personal identity and has no memory of how he traveled 100 km to reach the hospital. A collateral history reveals he experienced a traumatic earthquake a few months ago. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The patient's presentation is a classic example of a dissociative disorder, specifically dissociative amnesia with a dissociative fugue. The key features that point to this diagnosis are:
Inability to Recall Personal Information: The patient is unable to remember his personal identity and significant past events. This memory loss is not due to a medical condition.
Following a Traumatic Event: The amnesia is directly linked to a recent, severe traumatic stressor (the earthquake). This is a hallmark of dissociative disorders.
Dissociative Fugue: The patient's purposeful travel over a long distance (100 km) without any memory of the journey is characteristic of a dissociative fugue, a subtype of dissociative amnesia.
Dissociative identity disorder (Option B) involves the presence of two or more distinct personality states (or "alters"), which is not described in this case.
questions.subject: ent

questions.stem: A girl with schizophrenia presents to a Primary Health Centre (PHC) in India. Which of the following online applications, provided by the Government of India (GOI), is used for mental health service support? 101
explanation: Tele MANAS, which stands for Tele Mental Health Assistance and Networking Across States, is the correct answer. It is a landmark initiative by the Government of India (GOI) to provide mental health services to the public.
Service Offerings: Tele-MANAS is a 24/7, toll-free tele-mental health service designed to provide immediate mental health support to individuals in distress.

Accessibility: It is available through a toll-free number (14416) and is part of the National Mental Health Programme. It aims to bridge the gap in mental health care by providing accessible services, particularly in remote and underserved areas.
U-WIN (Option B) is an application developed by the government for immunization tracking, similar to the Co-WIN platform used for COVID-19 vaccination.
NIKUSHT(Option C) is a digital platform for the management and monitoring of sickle cell disease.
questions.subject: psychiatry

questions.stem: A 28-year-old software engineer presents with sleep disturbances, low mood, and stress for the past 3 months. He attributes his symptoms to ongoing problems at work. He has no prior psychiatric history. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? 102
explanation: The patient's symptoms are a classic presentation of adjustment disorder. The diagnostic criteria for this condition are:
Emotional or Behavioral Symptoms: The patient exhibits emotional or behavioral symptoms (sleep disturbances, low mood, stress) in response to an identifiable stressor.
Timeline: The symptoms must begin within three months of the onset of the stressor (in this case, excessive work pressure).

Marked Distress and Impairment: The symptoms cause marked distress that is disproportionate to the severity of the stressor and lead to significant impairment in social or occupational functioning (difficulty maintaining work-life balance).
Exclusion of Other Disorders: The symptoms do not meet the criteria for another specific mental disorder, such as major depressive disorder or a psychotic disorder.
questions.subject: ent

questions.stem: A patient presents with painful lid swelling. On examination, a pus point is observed at the base of an eyelash. Which of the following glands is involved?
explanation: Painful lid swelling with a visible opus point (localized yellowish spot of pus) at the base of an eyelash is characteristic of external hordeolum (stye).
An external hordeolum is an acute bacterial infection (usually Staphylococcus aureus) of the sebaceous glands of Zeis located at the eyelash follicle.
The pain is due to acute inflammation, and the pus may discharge spontaneously, relieving symptoms.
Condition Definition & Features Treatment External Hordeolum (Stye)
Acute suppurative inflammation of a lash follicle and associated glands of Zeis or
questions.subject: ophthalmology

questions.stem: A female presents with symptoms of being excessively talkative, hyperactive, sleeping very little, and spending large amounts of money on shopping sprees. When confronted, she becomes irritable. What is the most likely diagnosis? 105
explanation: The patient's symptoms are a classic and clear presentation of a manic episode, which is the defining characteristic of Bipolar I Disorder. The key symptoms described are:
Elevated or Irritable Mood: She is hyperactive and, when confronted, becomes irritable.
Decreased Need for Sleep: She is sleeping very little.
Pressured Speech: She is excessively talkative.
Risky or Impulsive Behavior: She is spending large amounts of money on shopping sprees, a form of impulsive and often reckless behavior seen in mania.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old patient presents with difficulty reacting to bright light. On examination, the pupillary light reflex is absent but the near reflex is preserved. Where is the lesion likely located?
explanation: Absence of the light reflex with preservation of the near reflex is known as light-near dissociation.

This occurs when the lesion interrupts the pathway from the optic tract to the Edinger– Westphal nucleus before the convergence–accommodation pathway branches — classically in the pretectal area of the midbrain.
Common causes: Argyll Robertson pupil (neurosyphilis), Parinaud’s syndrome (pineal tumors).
1. Light reflex pathway: Retina → optic nerve → optic chiasma → optic tract → pretectal area → bilateral Edinger–Westphal nuclei → oculomotor nerve → sphincter pupillae.
2. Near reflex pathway bypasses the pretectal area lesion and reaches Edinger–Westphal nuclei via cortical connections, so it remains intact.
questions.subject: physiology

questions.stem: A person has a false belief that he owns a lot of property and that someone he knows is trying to take it away from him. Which of the following best describes the type of delusions he is experiencing?
explanation: This patient is experiencing two distinct types of delusions simultaneously:

Delusion of Grandeur: This is a false and exaggerated belief in one's own importance, power, or identity. The belief that he owns a lot of property is an example of a grandiose delusion, as it exaggerates his financial or social status.

Delusion of Persecution: This is the most common type of delusion, characterized by the false belief that one is being harassed, harmed, or conspired against. The belief that someone is trying to take his property away from him is a classic example of a persecutory delusion.

Delusion of reference (Option B) involves the belief that neutral events or gestures are personally directed at oneself. For example, believing that a news reporter is speaking directly to them. This is not present in the scenario.

Delusion of nihilism (Options C and D) is the false belief that the self, a part of the self, or the world does not exist. This is not relevant to the patient's beliefs about property and being targeted.
questions.subject: psychiatry

questions.stem: A girl, after returning from a picnic, complains of pain in her eyes. She had not maintained proper hygiene of her contact lenses. What would be the cause?
explanation: Improper contact lens hygiene, especially with exposure to contaminated water (picnic scenario, possible swimming), is a major risk factor for Acanthamoeba keratitis.

Acanthamoeba is a free-living protozoan found in water, soil, and air; it can adhere to contact lenses and invade the corneal epithelium, causing severe pain (often disproportionate to clinical signs), redness, tearing, and photophobia.
Classic sign: ring-shaped stromal infiltrate in advanced cases.
Acanthamoeba keratitis:
1. Severe pain out of proportion to clinical findings.
questions.subject: ophthalmology

questions.stem: A patient with schizophrenia, developed muscular dystonia and was treated for it. The patient now presents with complaints of dry mouth, dizziness, and hypotension. What is the most likely drug causing these side effects? 111
explanation: The patient with schizophrenia likely developed acute dystonia as a side effect of antipsychotic therapy, which is commonly treated with an anticholinergic drug like benztropine.
Treatment: Muscular dystonia is typically treated with anticholinergic medications.
These drugs work by blocking acetylcholine activity to restore the balance of neurotransmitters in the brain.
New Symptoms: The patient now presents with dry mouth, dizziness, and hypotension. These are classic side effects of anticholinergic drugs.
Dry mouth: Caused by the anticholinergic effect of blocking muscarinic receptors in the salivary glands.
questions.subject: pharmacology

questions.stem: The following light reflex is seen in a patient. How much is the deviation?
explanation: The image shows the Hirschberg test result — the corneal light reflex is displaced toward the limbus in one eye.
In the Hirschberg test:
Each 1 mm displacement of the corneal light reflex from the center corresponds to ~15 prism diopters (PD) of deviation.
Reflex at the limbus ≈ 45 PD deviation.
Here, the reflex is at the corneal periphery/limbus, indicating about 45 PD of exotropia (outward deviation).
questions.subject: physiology

questions.stem: What could be the diagnosis for a child who exhibits below-average mathematical skills despite having a normal IQ and age-appropriate learning abilities and remains unaffected by a year-long private mathematics tutoring?
explanation: The child described is likely experiencing a Specific Learning Disorder (SLD), specifically with impairment in mathematics. This is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by persistent difficulties in learning and using academic skills despite normal intelligence, adequate educational opportunities, and appropriate interventions such as private tutoring.
Mathematical Difficulties: Problems with basic arithmetic operations, understanding numbers, or applying mathematical concepts.
Persistence: The issues persist despite normal IQ and adequate learning in other areas.
Impact on Daily Life: The difficulties impair academic achievement and daily functioning.

ADHD (Option A) involves inattention, hyperactivity, or impulsivity affecting multiple domains, including academics. Poor math performance in ADHD stems from difficulty focusing, not from specific deficits in mathematical skills.
questions.subject: paediatrics

questions.stem: A patient presents with a down and out right eye with ptosis since birth. What is the nerve palsy? 115
explanation: The down and out position of the eye indicates unopposed action of the lateral rectus (CN VI) and superior oblique (CN IV) muscles, suggesting paralysis of the other extraocular muscles supplied by oculomotor nerve (CN III).
Ptosis occurs due to paralysis of the levator palpebrae superioris (also supplied by CN
Since it is present since birth, this is likely a congenital oculomotor nerve palsy.
Key features of 3rd nerve palsy:
1. Eye position: abducted and depressed ("down and out").
questions.subject: anatomy

questions.stem: A posterior staphyloma was observed on indirect ophthalmoscopy. What is the likely diagnosis?
explanation: A posterior staphyloma is an outpouching of the weakened posterior sclera, visible as an ectatic area on fundus examination.
It occurs most commonly in pathological (high) myopia due to progressive elongation of the eyeball, leading to stretching and thinning of the sclera, choroid, and retina at the posterior pole.
This structural change can cause progressive visual decline and predispose to retinal degenerations/detachments.
Axial length usually >26 mm.
Fundus signs: temporal crescent, chorioretinal atrophy, lattice degeneration, and posterior staphyloma.

questions.stem: A patient presents with a history of dilation & curettage (D&C). Subsequent diagnostic tests reveal all hormone levels, including progesterone and estrogen, to be within the normal range. Based on this clinical presentation, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: A history of D&C followed by amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea with normal estrogen, progesterone, FSH, and LH levels points toward a uterine (outflow tract) cause.
Asherman syndrome results from intrauterine adhesions or fibrosis following procedures like D&C, leading to secondary amenorrhea despite normal ovarian function.
Since hormone production is normal, the problem lies in the endometrial cavity’s inability to respond due to scarring.
Asherman Syndrome:
1. Cause: Trauma to the basal layer of the endometrium (often post-D&C, postpartum curettage, or infection).
questions.subject: physiology

questions.stem: A female presents with multiple small, pink, umbilicated papules on the face. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The description of the skin lesions—multiple small, pink, umbilicated papules—is the classic clinical presentation of Molluscum contagiosum.
Etiology: This is a common, self-limiting viral skin infection caused by a poxvirus.
Lesion Characteristics: The lesions are typically flesh-colored or pink, dome-shaped papules with a characteristic central umbilication (a small indentation or dimple in the center).
Affected Population: It is most common in children, particularly on the face, trunk, and extremities, but can also be seen in sexually active adults in the genital area.
Herpes simplex (Option B) presents as grouped vesicles (small blisters) on an erythematous base, not umbilicated papules.

questions.stem: Identify the given condition and its treatment. 124
questions.option_a: Staphylococcal scalded syndrome with Inj cephalexin
explanation: The image shows a child with widespread erythema and superficial desquamation (peeling of the skin). This is the hallmark of Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (SSSS).

Pathophysiology: SSSS is caused by an exfoliative toxin produced by Staphylococcus aureus. This toxin causes the top layer of the epidermis to detach, leading to the characteristic blistering and peeling of the skin, making it look "scalded."

Treatment: As it is a serious systemic infection, the treatment involves intravenous (IV) antistaphylococcal antibiotics to target the underlying bacterial cause. The skin itself is treated with supportive care, similar to that for a burn patient. The provided answer, while mentioning cephalexin (an oral antibiotic), likely refers to the class of antistaphylococcal antibiotics required.

Epidermolysis bullosa (Option B) is a group of rare genetic disorders that cause the skin to be fragile and blister easily. It is not an infectious condition and is managed with supportive care, but it is not the primary diagnosis here.

Impetigo bullosa (Option C) is a localized form of staphylococcal skin infection that presents with large blisters (bullae). It does not typically cause the widespread erythema and peeling seen in SSSS.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the characteristic lesion of Kaposi sarcoma?
questions.option_b: A white, striated lesion that cannot be scraped off
explanation: The characteristic lesion of Kaposi sarcoma (KS) is a raised, purple-red lesion.
Etiology: Kaposi sarcoma is a type of cancer caused by the Human Herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8).

Lesion Morphology: The lesions typically begin as flat, reddish-purple macules and progress into raised, palpable plaques and nodules. The color is a result of extravasated red blood cells and hemosiderin deposition. The violaceous (purple-red) appearance is a hallmark of this condition.
Affected Population: It is most commonly seen in patients with compromised immune systems, particularly those with HIV/AIDS.
A yellow lesion containing pus (Option A) would suggest a bacterial infection, like a carbuncle or abscess.
questions.subject: dermatology

questions.stem: A child accidentally took a bottle full of iron tablets. Which of the drugs is used as an antidote for iron poisoning?
explanation: Acute iron poisoning can occur when a person ingests a toxic amount of iron tablets or other iron-containing substances.
Deferoxamine is a chelating agent that is used as the antidote for Acute iron poisoning.
Mechanism: Binds iron in the body and forms a stable complex that can be excreted through urine and faeces.
Deferoxamine helps to remove excess iron from the body and prevent its toxic effects.
Luspatercept (Option B) is a medication used for the treatment of anemia in certain blood disorders and is not indicated for the treatment of iron poisoning.
questions.subject: pharmacology

questions.stem: A patient with borderline tuberculoid leprosy presents with the following findings. Which type of hypersensitivity reaction and nerve is enlarged? 132
explanation: The image shows a patient with a visibly thickened and enlarged nerve in the neck region, which is the greater auricular nerve. The question also mentions that the patient has borderline tuberculoid leprosy and is experiencing a reaction.

Leprosy Reactions: Leprosy reactions are episodes of acute inflammation that occur during the course of the disease. Type 1 (reversal) reactions are a form of delayed-type hypersensitivity (Type IV) reaction. They are caused by a sudden increase in the cell- mediated immune response to the Mycobacterium leprae bacteria.

Clinical Presentation: These reactions often involve inflammation of existing skin lesions and, most importantly, acute neuritis, which is the painful inflammation and enlargement of peripheral nerves. The greater auricular nerve is a common site for this nerve enlargement.
Therefore, the correct pairing is a Type 4 hypersensitivity reaction and the greater auricular nerve.
Type 1 & greater auricular nerve (Option A) is an immediate, IgE-mediated reaction (e.g., an allergic reaction), which is not what causes leprosy reactions.
questions.subject: anatomy

questions.stem: A child was brought with c/o multiple lesions. O/E multiple vesicular lesions are seen on the palms, soles, and oral mucosa. Which of the following is the most likely etiological agent causing the disease? 134
explanation: The most likely etiological agent causing multiple vesicular lesions on the palms, soles, and oral mucosa in a child is Coxsackie virus.
The classic presentation described—vesicular lesions on the hands, feet, and oral mucosa—is characteristic of Hand, Foot, and Mouth Disease (HFMD).

HFMD is most commonly caused by Coxsackievirus A16 and occasionally other coxsackievirus strains (like A6, A10) and Enterovirus 71. However, Coxsackievirus remains the prototypical agent linked to these clinical findings in children.
Varicella-zoster virus (Option A) Causes chickenpox, which presents with a generalized vesicular rash, mostly on the trunk and face.
Human herpesvirus 6 (Option B) Most known for roseola, characterized by a sudden fever followed by a maculopapular rash, not vesicular and not localized to palms/soles/oral mucosa.
questions.subject: paediatrics

questions.stem: Oral contraceptive pill prevents all except?
explanation: OCPs have documented protective effects against epithelial ovarian cancer and endometrial cancer by suppressing ovulation and providing a progestin-dominant environment.
Some studies also suggest a modest protective effect against colorectal cancer.
However, long-term OCP use is associated with a slightly increased risk of cervical cancer due to hormonal influence on HPV persistence.
OCP cancer protection profile:
1. Protective against: Epithelial ovarian, endometrial, and possibly colorectal cancer.

questions.stem: A patient presents to the dermatology clinic reporting recurrent skin lesions as seen on the image. He describes that these lesions emerge within hours each time he takes NSAIDs. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Fixed drug eruption features:
Recurrent lesions that appear within hours of taking NSAIDs
well-demarcated, erythematous patch
They recur at the same site upon re-exposure to the offending drug
Common triggers: NSAIDs, sulfa drugs, antibiotics
questions.subject: dermatology

questions.stem: What is the importance of the following manoeuvre?
questions.option_a: To protect from tearing of the perineum
questions.option_b: To pull the baby out faster
questions.option_c: To facilitate controlled extension of the fetal head
questions.option_d: To rotate the shoulders during delivery
explanation: This image depicts the classic Ritgen/perineal support manoeuvre — one hand supports the fetal head while the other applies controlled counter-pressure through the perineum to the fetal chin/posterior neck. Its primary purpose is to regulate the speed and angle of head extension during crowning, thereby reducing sudden expulsion and minimizing perineal and neonatal trauma. The main aim is to protect the perineum from sudden overstretching and tearing.
Performed at crowning to control the final extension of the fetal head rather than hasten delivery.
One hand supports the head externally; the other applies upward/posterior counter- pressure through the perineum on the fetal chin/posterior neck to moderate extension.
Benefits: slows uncontrolled delivery of the head, decreases downward force on the perineum, reduces risk/severity of perineal tears and neonatal scalp trauma.
Not intended to speed delivery; it is a protective, controlled manoeuvre.

questions.stem: Identify the procedure. 144
explanation: Hegar's sign is elicited by bimanual examination in early pregnancy (around 6–8 weeks), revealing a softening of the lower uterine segment between the cervix and the uterine body. This softening allows the cervix and uterine body to feel like two separate structures.
Softening occurs due to hormonal effects (increased vascularity and edema from estrogen/progesterone).
Detected by gently pressing the cervix from below (per vaginam) and the uterine fundus from above (per abdomen).
A probable sign of pregnancy (not diagnostic).
Helps distinguish early pregnancy from other uterine enlargements.

questions.stem: A patient presents with severe headache. A CT scan of the brain reveals hypodense areas in the right basal region, marked as ‘X’. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? 148
explanation: Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) typically presents with a sudden, severe “thunderclap” headache, often described as the worst headache of life.
On non-contrast CT, acute blood in the subarachnoid space appears hyperdense (bright), especially in the basal cisterns, Sylvian fissures, and interhemispheric fissure.
When the blood fills the basal cisterns, the appearance is classically described as the “Star of Death” sign.
Hypodense areas may develop later due to ischemia or edema.
The most common cause is rupture of a saccular (berry) aneurysm at the circle of Willis.
questions.subject: radiology

questions.stem: The following radiological image was taken to assess the urinary tract. Identify the investigation shown below. 153
explanation: An Intravenous pyelogram (IVP) is a contrast-based radiological investigation used to visualize the anatomy and function of the urinary tract, including kidneys, ureters, and bladder.

Iodinated contrast is injected intravenously, filtered by the kidneys, and excreted into the collecting system, allowing sequential imaging to assess drainage and detect structural abnormalities such as calculi, strictures, or masses.
The image typically shows contrast-filled calyces, pelvis, ureters, and bladder in a single study.
Pelvic X-ray (Option B): Plain film without contrast; only bony pelvis and gross calcifications visible.
Retrograde pyelogram (Option C): Contrast introduced via catheter through the urethra and bladder directly into ureters; used if IV contrast is contraindicated.

questions.stem: Identify the tarsal bone marked by the arrow in the given X-ray of the right foot. 157
explanation: The navicular bone is a wedge-shaped tarsal bone located medially in the midfoot.
It articulates proximally with the talus, distally with the three cuneiform bones, and laterally with the cuboid.
On a dorsoplantar (DP) or oblique foot X-ray, it is seen on the medial side between the talus and cuneiforms.
The arrow in this case points to its distinctive position and shape.
Cuboid (Option B): Found on the lateral aspect of the foot between the calcaneus and 4th–5th metatarsals.

questions.stem: Identify the investigation shown in the image:
explanation: The image shows the two standard mammographic views used in breast cancer screening and diagnosis:
Mediolateral Oblique (MLO) view – includes the upper outer quadrant and pectoralis muscle.
Craniocaudal (CC) view – top-down view of the breast.
Mammography is performed using low-dose X-rays with breast compression to improve resolution and detect abnormalities such as masses, architectural distortions, and microcalcifications.

questions.stem: Identify the condition shown in the chest X-ray of a sand mine worker. What is the most likely diagnosis? 161
explanation: Silicosis is a pneumoconiosis caused by chronic inhalation of crystalline silica dust, common in occupations such as sand mining, stone cutting, and quarrying.

Inhaled silica particles are phagocytosed by alveolar macrophages, leading to their death and the release of inflammatory mediators, which stimulate fibroblast proliferation and collagen deposition, resulting in progressive pulmonary fibrosis.
On chest X-ray, the hallmark finding is "eggshell calcification" — a thin rim of calcification along the periphery of enlarged hilar lymph nodes.
Parenchymal findings often include small rounded opacities (upper lobe–predominant nodules) that may coalesce over time, forming progressive massive fibrosis.
Sarcoidosis (Option A): Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy without eggshell calcification; may show parenchymal reticulonodular pattern.

questions.stem: What does a linear accelerator (LINAC) produce in radiation therapy?
explanation: A linear accelerator (LINAC) is the most commonly used device in external beam radiation therapy.
It accelerates electrons to high energies using microwave technology.
These high-energy electrons can be used directly to treat superficial tumors, or they can be directed onto a metal target to produce high-energy X-rays (photons) for treating deep- seated tumors.
This allows precise targeting of malignant tissue while sparing surrounding normal structures.
The overhead X-ray tube emits X-ray beams to the patient.

questions.stem: Fetal anaemia is primarily determined by Doppler assessment of which artery?
explanation: Fetal anemia is best assessed by Doppler measurement of the peak systolic velocity (PSV) in the Middle Cerebral Artery (MCA).
In fetal anemia, the blood’s oxygen-carrying capacity is reduced, so the fetus compensates with increased cardiac output and decreased blood viscosity.
This leads to faster blood flow in the MCA, which can be picked up on Doppler.
An MCA-PSV > 1.5 multiples of the median (MoM) for gestational age strongly suggests moderate-to-severe anemia.
Other vessels like the umbilical artery or ductus venosus are more useful for assessing placental insufficiency or cardiac compromise, not specifically anemia.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old primigravida presents with painful vaginal bleeding in the first trimester. On USG, a well-formed gestation ring with central echoes from the embryo indicates a healthy fetus, and there is observation of fetal cardiac motion. What is the most probable diagnosis?
explanation: The presence of vaginal bleeding in the first trimester along with a healthy fetus observed on USG, indicated by a well-formed gestation ring, central echoes from the embryo, and fetal cardiac motion, is characteristic of a threatened abortion. The cervix is typically closed in such cases, and the pregnancy may still continue successfully with appropriate Clinical Classification of Spontaneous Abortion Characteristic Threatened Inevitable (Option Incomplete (Option C) Complete (Option D) Missed Presentation Vaginal bleeding in < 20 weeks Vaginal bleeding membrane rupture in Expulsion of some products of conception Cervix closed after conception product expulsion symptoms Cervical OS Closed Open Open Closed Closed USG Findings Normal intrauterine pregnancy with fetal heartbeat Dilated cervix with sac low in the uterine cavity or cervical gestational sac or retained products of conception uterus with no retained products of conception Absence of heartbeat and/or absent growth of gestational Management Rest, monitor viability Prepare inevitable expulsion Evacuation of retained products of conception Observation Surgical evacuation or expectant management
